items.q: What is the mode of the data set [1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5]?
items.correct: Both 2 and 4 — the data set is bimodal because two values occur most frequently.
items.misconception: There is no mode because no single value is the most common.
items.random_wrong: The mode is 3 because it's the middle value.
items.why: Students assume the mode must be unique and don't recognise that a data set can have more than one mode (or none).

items.q: What is the magnitude of the vector (3, 4)?
items.misconception: The magnitude is 7 — you add the components.
items.random_wrong: The magnitude is 12 — you multiply the components.
items.why: Students don't connect vector magnitude with Pythagoras; they treat vectors as ordered pairs to add or multiply directly.

items.q: Does the limit of f(x) as x approaches a have to equal f(a)?
items.correct: No — the limit can exist even if f(a) is undefined or different from the limit.
items.misconception: The limit is always just the value of f at that point — that's how you compute it.
items.random_wrong: Limits always equal zero unless the function is constant.
items.why: Students learn limits by substituting (for continuous functions) and don't realise limits and function values are conceptually distinct.
Correct answer: both a and b. “only a” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: parallelogram area. “dot product” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
